title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Lansing's new moratorium and tech firms agreeing to pay for grid upgrades reshape Michigan's data center future.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're digging into why Lansing just hit the brakes on new proposals and how that fits into the bigger fight over who pays for all this power.
Yesterday the Lansing City Council voted to enact a 182-day moratorium on any new data center proposals.
That's a pretty firm pause until January 2027. What exactly does it block, Graham?

It stops the city from even considering new projects while officials work on permanent rules. The move comes amid growing local concerns about power demand, water use, and how these facilities fit into neighborhoods.
Why now? It feels like we've seen a wave of these proposals lately.
Exactly. Michigan has become a hot spot for data centers because of available land, fiber connections, and relatively affordable power compared to some coastal states.
But that power isn't free. Who's been footing the bill for the upgrades so far?

That's the core tension. Utilities like DTE and Consumers Energy have been planning big transmission investments, and there's been real worry that everyday ratepayers would end up covering a lot of those costs through higher electric bills.
And data centers can use enormous amounts of electricity. How much are we talking?

A single large campus can demand hundreds of megawatts, sometimes approaching a gigawatt. That's enough to power a small city, and it runs 24/7 with massive cooling needs that also pull a lot of water.
So farmland gets converted, noise becomes an issue for neighbors, and towns have to figure out roads and schools. What's the history here in Michigan?

Over the past couple of years we've seen proposals pop up from Saline Township to Negaunee, Manchester, and plenty of spots in between. Some communities pushed back hard on tax incentives and zoning, while others welcomed the jobs and tax base.
And the state has been trying to speed things up too, right?

Yes. On Sunday Governor Whitmer rolled out the Michigan Affordable and Responsible Growth Action Plan that sets standards for siting, power, and water. Companies that don't follow the rules could lose state incentives.
How does Lansing's moratorium play into that state effort?

It gives the city breathing room to write its own rules instead of reacting to whatever comes in next. Other townships are watching closely because what happens in the capital often influences the rest of the state.
What happens next for Lansing residents specifically?

Council members will spend the next six months studying impacts on the grid, water systems, and local services. They'll likely hold public meetings to gather input before adopting permanent zoning or incentive policies.
And for the broader state picture, does this slow things down everywhere?

Not necessarily everywhere, but it signals that local pushback is getting organized. We've already seen Mason residents raising money to fight a project and Saline Township dropping its cap on tax breaks after earlier resistance.
That Saline move opens the door to bigger incentives for a project that could pull hundreds of megawatts. How does that square with the ratepayer protections we're hearing about today?

Today we also learned that tech giants have agreed to pay for power infrastructure upgrades, and Michigan officials secured commitments aimed at shielding households from the full cost of new transmission lines.
That's a shift. Who announced that?
State officials confirmed the agreements amid rising power demand from these facilities. It could set a template for how future projects get funded across both DTE and Consumers Energy territories.
Meanwhile the state GOP chair is floating a ban on utility deals that would otherwise shift data-center costs onto regular customers. How might that interact with the tech-company commitments?

The chair said he may back legislation stopping utilities from shielding data centers from rate hikes. If it passes, it would directly affect how billions in grid upgrades get paid for, with the biggest impact on ratepayers in both utility service areas.
So even with the new agreements, there's still political pressure to lock in protections. What about local tax fights?

Yesterday Manchester granted a scaled-back tax break after the original ask was cut by 89 percent. The project still gets some relief, but residents in Manchester and Washtenaw County will see less revenue than developers first sought, locking in long-term effects for schools and services.
And Negaunee has a hearing coming up on zoning rules. When is that again?

August 13 in Marquette County. Residents there will weigh in on proposed ordinances that could shape where and how large data centers locate, directly affecting land use, power demand, and the local tax base.
Utility allies are also going after a vocal critic of large projects. What's behind those attacks?

Groups linked to Consumers Energy have targeted the opponent as utilities face scrutiny over rising power costs tied to new server farms. Michigan residents could see higher bills if projects move forward without checks.
And there's that township pushing back against state rules limiting its ability to block centers. How does that fit the pattern?

Residents there worry about noise, power strain, and water use from projects that could arrive despite local opposition. Whitmer's growth plan aims to speed approvals, which raises the stakes for communities wanting more say.
With all these moving pieces, what's the one thing listeners should watch in the coming weeks?

How other cities and townships react to Lansing's moratorium. If more places follow suit, it could force a broader conversation at the state level about balancing growth with local control and ratepayer protection.
Thanks for laying that out. Let's move to a few quick hits from the last day or so.

Today Saline Township removed its proposed ceiling on tax incentives for a power-hungry server farm after earlier resistance. That opens the door to bigger breaks for a project that could reshape local tax rolls and affect residents 30 minutes away through school funding and water demand.
Also today, tech giants agreed to pay for power infrastructure while Michigan works to shield ratepayers from the full bill for new transmission lines and upgrades.
And the state GOP chair floated possible legislation to ban utility deals that would otherwise pass data-center costs onto households.
Yesterday a local township continued its battle against state data center rules even as Whitmer unveiled her new growth pledge aimed at speeding approvals.
Utility allies went after a vocal data-center critic, and Manchester granted a sharply reduced tax break for a project after the original request was cut by nearly 90 percent.
Negaunee set an August 13 hearing on new zoning rules, and Mason residents are raising cash to fight a proposed facility in Ingham County.
On deck, the Negaunee hearing on August 13 is one residents in Marquette County can attend to weigh in on data-center zoning. We'll keep an eye on any new public meetings that pop up this week.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Lansing Pauses Data Centers as Power Costs and Local Control Clash Statewide
